segments.inferno-2-g149: “Or” is an apocopated form of “ora,” meaning “now,” and “va” is the second-person singular imperative of “andare.” The brief command urges Virgil to proceed.
segments.inferno-2-g150: “ch’” introduces the reason for the command, here meaning “for/because.” “Un sol volere” means “one single will,” with the noun “volere” used substantively; “d’ambedue” means “of both,” i.e. shared by both speakers.
segments.inferno-2-g151: This is an elliptical nominal clause: “tu duca” means “you [are] guide,” with the verb “are” omitted for poetic force. “Duca” here is not a noble title but the one who leads the way.
segments.inferno-2-g152: The ellipsis continues: “tu [sei] segnore e tu [sei] maestro.” The repeated “tu” emphasizes Dante’s full submission to Virgil as lord and master/teacher.
segments.inferno-2-g153: “Così” means “thus,” referring back to the preceding words Dante has just spoken. “Li” is an archaic indirect object pronoun meaning “to him,” and “dissi” is the first-person singular preterite of “dire,” “I said.”
segments.inferno-2-g154: “Poi che” functions together as a temporal conjunction meaning “after” or “when.” “Mosso fue” is an archaic, inverted compound form, literally “was moved,” meaning “had moved/set out,” with “fue” as an old form of “fu.”
segments.inferno-2-g155: “Intrai” is the first-person singular preterite, “I entered.” “Per lo cammino” means “upon/through the path,” with “lo” as the older article form; “alto e silvestro” characterizes the path as deep/lofty and wild or woodland-like.
segments.inferno-2-g32: The conjunction “o” introduces an alternative question, continuing the speaker’s doubt. “’l” is an elided object pronoun, short for “lo,” meaning “it,” placed before the present-tense verb “concede”: literally, “who grants it?”
segments.inferno-2-g33: “Io non Enëa” is an elliptical clause: the verb “sono” is understood from the following segment. The named comparison, Aeneas, emphasizes the speaker’s lack of heroic qualification.
segments.inferno-2-g34: Here the full predicate appears: “io non Paulo sono,” with poetic word order placing the complement “Paulo” before “sono.” The meaning is “I am not Paul,” another example of someone who had a divinely sanctioned journey to the other world.
segments.inferno-2-g35: “Me degno a ciò” is fronted for emphasis: literally, “me worthy of this.” The paired negatives “né io né altri” mean “neither I nor anyone else,” and “’l crede” uses elided “lo” with “crede” in the sense of “thinks/deems it so.”
segments.inferno-2-g36: “Per che” is a two-word form meaning “wherefore,” “therefore,” or “for which reason.” It links this sentence as a conclusion drawn from the speaker’s sense of unworthiness.
segments.inferno-2-g37: Here “se” means “if,” though it is not the contracted verb “se’.” “Del venire” uses the infinitive “venire” as a noun, “the coming” or “the act of going,” and “m’abbandono” is reflexive: “I give myself over / commit myself.”
segments.inferno-2-g38: “Temo che” introduces a feared possibility and therefore takes the subjunctive “sia.” The “non” is pleonastic or expletive after a verb of fearing, so the sense is not “may not be rash,” but “may be rash.”
segments.inferno-2-g39: “Se’” is the contracted second-person singular of “essere,” meaning “you are.” “Savio” is the predicate adjective, directly addressing Virgil as wise or discerning.
segments.inferno-2-g40: “Intendi” is an imperative or present form addressed to Virgil: “understand.” “Me’” is an archaic shortened form of “meglio,” meaning “better,” not the pronoun “me”; “ch’” introduces a comparison, and the “non” before “ragiono” is pleonastic, giving the sense “better than I speak/explain.”
segments.inferno-2-g138: This target consists only of a full stop, marking the end of the preceding speech. There are no lexical items to analyze here.
segments.inferno-2-g139: “Quali” introduces the first half of a comparison, answered later by “tal” (“as ... so”). “Dal notturno gelo” gives the cause or agent of the participles “chinati e chiusi”: the little flowers are bent and closed by the night frost.
segments.inferno-2-g140: “Poi che” functions together as an archaic/poetic temporal conjunction meaning “after” or “when after.” “’l” is the elided article before “sol,” here meaning “sun,” while “li” is a direct-object pronoun meaning “them”; “’mbianca” is a poetic form of “imbianca,” “whitens.”
segments.inferno-2-g141: The subject is still “fioretti,” and “si drizzan” is a shortened poetic form of “si drizzano,” “they straighten themselves.” “Tutti aperti” describes their resulting state, while “in loro stelo” means on or upon their own stalks/stems.
segments.inferno-2-g142: “Tal” completes the comparison begun by “Quali”: “so / such did I...” The order is poetic: “mi fec’ io” means “I made myself” or “I became,” and “di mia virtude stanca” indicates the condition from which he changed—his weary strength or courage.
segments.inferno-2-g143: This clause continues the result of the simile: courage enters him. “Mi corse al cor” is poetic word order for “ran/came to my heart,” with “mi” giving a personal/dative nuance, “to me” or “within me.”
segments.inferno-2-g144: “Ch’i’” is a contraction of “che io,” here introducing the result: “so much courage came ... that I began.” “Cominciai” means “I began speaking,” and “come persona franca” means like a free, confident, bold person.
segments.inferno-2-g145: This begins Dante’s direct speech with an exclamatory, elliptical construction: “Oh, compassionate [was] she...” “Colei che mi soccorse” means “she who helped me,” with “soccorse” as a past-tense form referring to Beatrice’s aid.
segments.inferno-2-g146: This continues the exclamation begun in the previous sentence: Dante praises both the woman who helped him and Virgil, addressed as “te cortese.” “Ch’ubidisti” is “che ubidisti,” a relative clause meaning “who obeyed/heeded,” and “che ti porse” refers to the true words that she offered to Virgil.
segments.inferno-2-g147: The subject “Tu” is emphatic: Dante is addressing Virgil directly. “M’hai ... il cor disposto” uses “avere” as the auxiliary in a perfect tense, with “m’” giving a dative-of-interest or possessive sense: “you have disposed my heart.” “Sì al venir” means “so toward coming,” and “con le parole tue” explains the means—by Virgil’s words.
segments.inferno-2-g148: “Ch’i’” means “that I,” introducing the result of the previous “sì”: “so ... that I...” “Son tornato” is a perfect tense with “essere,” and “nel primo proposto” means “to/into my former purpose,” namely the original decision to undertake the journey.
segments.inferno-2-g80: “Tacette” is a third-person singular past-tense form meaning “she fell silent.” “Allora” marks the next action in the narrative, “then.”
segments.inferno-2-g81: “Comincia’” is the poetic contracted form of “cominciai,” first-person singular preterite, “I began.” The subject pronoun “io” follows the verb for emphasis and to fit the verse, while “e poi” links this action after her silence.
segments.inferno-2-g82: This is a vocative address to the lady: “O donna di virtù.” “Sola per cui” means “you alone through whom,” and the relative clause continues with “l’umana spezie eccede,” where the subject is “the human species/race.” “C’ha” is an elision of “che ha,” and “li cerchi sui” means “its circles,” with archaic “li” for “i” and “sui” for “suoi.”
segments.inferno-2-g83: “Tanto” introduces a result construction completed by the following “che”: “so much ... that.” “M’aggrada” means “pleases me,” with “m’” standing for “mi,” and the grammatical subject is “il tuo comandamento.”
segments.inferno-2-g84: “Che” introduces the result clause dependent on “tanto.” “L’ubidir” is a substantivized infinitive, literally “the obeying,” meaning “the act of obeying.”
segments.inferno-2-g85: “Se già fosse” is a compact conditional clause: “even if it were already [done].” “Fosse” is an imperfect subjunctive of “essere,” used after “se” for a hypothetical condition.
segments.inferno-2-g86: “M’è tardi” is idiomatic: literally “it is late to me,” meaning “it seems late to me” or “I feel it is already delayed.” The dative pronoun “m’” marks the person affected by the feeling.
segments.inferno-2-g87: “Più non” gives the sense “no more” or “no longer.” “T’è uo’” is an archaic expression from “uopo,” meaning “there is need for you”; the clause says that she need only “aprirmi il tuo talento,” literally “open your desire to me,” that is, reveal her wish.
segments.inferno-2-g88: “Dimmi” is the imperative “tell” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi,” “tell me.” “Che non ti guardi” depends on “la cagion” and means “why you do not guard yourself/beware,” with “ti” reflexive. “De lo scender” uses the infinitive as a noun, “the descending,” and “ove tornar tu ardi” means “where you burn to return,” with “ardi” expressing intense desire.
segments.inferno-2-g89: “Da che” means “since” or “given that,” literally something like “from the fact that.” “Vuo’” is the contracted second-person singular of “vuoi,” and “saver” is the apocopated infinitive “sapere.” “Cotanto a dentro” means “so far inwardly,” hence “so deeply.”
segments.inferno-2-g90: “Dirotti” is not the adjective “dirotto” here but a fused form of “dirò” plus “ti”: “I will tell you.” “Brievemente” modifies that future verb, “briefly.”
segments.inferno-2-g91: “Mi rispuose” means “she answered me.” “Rispuose” is an archaic or poetic preterite form corresponding to modern “rispose.”
segments.inferno-2-g92: “Perch’” here means “why,” introducing the indirect question that she will explain. “I’” is a poetic contraction of “io,” and “venir qua entro” means “to come in here,” with “entro” reinforcing inward motion.
segments.inferno-2-g93: “Temer si dee” is an impersonal construction: “one must fear” or “one ought to fear.” “Di sole quelle cose” means “only those things,” and the relative clause “c’hanno potenza” has “c’” as elided “che,” not the pronoun “ci.” “Di fare altrui male” means “to do harm to others.”
segments.inferno-2-g94: This is an elliptical phrase: “de l’altre no” means “of the others, no,” that is, one need not fear the other things. “De l’” is a contraction of “de” plus the article before “altre.”
segments.inferno-2-g95: “Ché” gives the reason: “because” or “for.” “Son” is the shortened form of “sono,” and “paurose” here means “fearsome/frightening,” not “timid.”
segments.inferno-2-g1: “Lo giorno” uses the older masculine article “lo” where modern Italian would normally have “il.” “Se n’andava” is a pronominal expression: the reflexive-like “se” plus “ne” gives the sense of going away or departing, and “andava” is imperfect, describing the evening as in progress.
segments.inferno-2-g2: This clause continues the imperfect description with “toglieva,” whose object is “li animai,” an archaic form for “gli animali” or living creatures. “Da le fatiche loro” means “from their labors,” with “loro” placed after the noun in an older/poetic possessive order.
segments.inferno-2-g3: “Io sol uno” is emphatic: Dante contrasts himself alone with all creatures being relieved from labor. “M’apparecchiava a sostener” uses the imperfect reflexive, “I was preparing myself to endure,” and “sì ... e sì ...” coordinates the two kinds of struggle: that of the journey and that of pity.
segments.inferno-2-g4: “Che” is relative, referring back to the “guerra,” the struggle just mentioned. “Ritrarrà” is future tense, “will recount/depict,” and “la mente che non erra” means the reliable memory or mind that does not make mistakes.
segments.inferno-2-g5: “O” is the vocative particle, used to invoke the Muses directly. “Muse” refers to the classical goddesses of poetic inspiration, whom the poet calls upon at the beginning of this demanding passage.
segments.inferno-2-g6: This is another vocative phrase parallel to “O muse.” “Alto ingegno” means a lofty or elevated intellectual power, with “alto” used figuratively rather than physically.
segments.inferno-2-g7: “Or” means “now,” introducing the request after the invocations. “M’aiutate” is “mi aiutate,” with elision before a vowel; “aiutate” functions as an imperative addressed to the invoked powers.
segments.inferno-2-g8: “O mente” is a further apostrophe, addressed to Dante’s own memory or mind. “Scrivesti” is second-person singular preterite, “you wrote/recorded,” and “ciò ch’io vidi” means “that which I saw,” with “ch’” elided before “io.”
segments.inferno-2-g9: “Qui” means “here,” that is, at this point in the poem or in this undertaking. “Si parrà” is a reflexive/impersonal-looking future form meaning “will show itself” or “will become evident,” with “la tua nobilitate” as the subject placed after the verb.
segments.inferno-2-g10: “Io cominciai” introduces Dante’s direct speech. “Cominciai” is first-person singular passato remoto, the narrative past tense typical of the poem.
segments.inferno-2-g11: “Poeta” is a direct address to Virgil. The relative clause “che mi guidi” means “who guide me,” where “mi” is the object of “guidi.”
segments.inferno-2-g12: “Guarda” is an imperative meaning “look at” or “consider.” “S’ell’ è” is an elided form of “se ella è,” here meaning “whether it is,” with “ella” referring back to “la mia virtù,” Dante’s strength or capacity.
segments.inferno-2-g13: “Prima ch’” introduces a temporal clause, “before,” and in older Italian it can be followed by a verb form like “fidi.” “Tu mi fidi” means “you entrust me,” and “a l’alto passo” gives the destination or ordeal to which Dante would be entrusted; “alto” is figurative, suggesting a grave or difficult passage.
segments.inferno-2-g14: “Tu dici che” introduces Dante’s report of what Virgil has said or written. “Di Silvïo il parente” has poetic inverted word order: literally “of Silvius the parent,” meaning Silvius’s father, Aeneas.
segments.inferno-2-g15: This adjective phrase modifies “il parente.” “Corruttibile ancora” means “still corruptible,” that is, still mortal and in a perishable body.
segments.inferno-2-g16: “Ad immortale secolo” means “to the immortal world/realm,” referring to the world beyond mortal life. “Andò” is passato remoto, the simple narrative past: “he went.”
segments.inferno-2-g17: “E fu sensibilmente” is elliptical: literally “and he was sensibly/perceptibly,” meaning he was there in a bodily, sense-perceptible state. “Fu” thus implies presence there, not merely existence in the abstract.
segments.inferno-2-g18: “Però” here has its older consequential force, “therefore” or “for that reason,” introducing the conclusion Dante draws from the preceding example. It links the whole long conditional explanation that follows to the prior mention of Aeneas’s journey.
segments.inferno-2-g19: “Se” means “if,” though that sense is not in the supplied list; it opens a conditional clause. “Cortese i fu” uses poetic word order: the adjective “cortese” precedes the verb, and “i” is an archaic dative pronoun meaning “to him,” so the phrase means “was gracious/generous to him.”
segments.inferno-2-g20: “Pensando” is a gerund, “considering” or “bearing in mind,” explaining why such favor was fitting. “Ch’uscir dovea di lui” is a relative clause: “that was to issue from him,” with “dovea” as an archaic imperfect of “dovere” expressing destiny or future-in-the-past.
segments.inferno-2-g21: “’l chi e ’l quale” substantivizes the interrogatives: literally “the who and the what-kind,” meaning “who he was and what he was.” “Non pare indegno ad omo d’intelletto” has the impersonal-seeming construction “does not seem unworthy to a man of understanding,” with “omo” as an archaic form of “uomo.”
segments.inferno-2-g22: “Ch’” here is causal, “for/because,” giving the reason Aeneas’s privilege was appropriate. “E’” is not the conjunction “and” but an archaic pronoun “he,” and “fu ... eletto” is a passive perfect: “he was chosen”; “per padre” means “as/to be father.”
segments.inferno-2-g23: “La quale e ’l quale” are relative pronouns referring back respectively to Rome and the empire. The repeated article plus “quale” is the normal medieval Italian form for “which.”
segments.inferno-2-g24: “A voler dir lo vero” is an idiomatic infinitive phrase meaning “if one wishes to tell the truth” or “to tell the truth.” “Lo vero” uses the article with an adjective used substantively, meaning “the true thing,” hence “the truth.”
segments.inferno-2-g25: “Fu stabilita” is a passive perfect, “was established,” with the feminine singular form attracted especially to “Roma,” though the sense includes both Rome and empire. “U’” is an archaic shortened form of “where,” introducing the relative clause “where the successor of the greater Peter sits,” meaning the papal seat.
segments.inferno-2-g26: “Per quest’ andata” means “through/by this going,” with “andata” used as a noun for the journey. “Onde li dai tu vanto” means “for which you give him praise”; “li” is an archaic dative “to him,” and “dai” is the verb “you give,” despite the supplied lemma treating it as a prepositional contraction.
segments.inferno-2-g27: “Intese” is a past-tense verb meaning “he learned” or “came to know.” The relative clause “che furon cagione” modifies “cose,” and “di sua vittoria e del papale ammanto” gives the two results: his victory and the papal mantle or dignity.
segments.inferno-2-g28: “Andovvi” is a compact archaic form: “andò” plus “vi,” meaning “he went there.” “Lo Vas d’elezïone” is the biblical title “the Vessel of Election,” referring to the chosen apostolic instrument; “lo” is the older article form before this noun.
segments.inferno-2-g29: “Per recarne conforto” is a purpose phrase: “in order to bring comfort from it,” with “-ne” referring back to that heavenly journey or vision. “Ch’è principio a la via di salvazione” is a relative clause modifying “fede”: faith is the beginning of the way to salvation.
segments.inferno-2-g30: “Ma io” sets up a contrast: after naming figures worthy of such journeys, the speaker turns abruptly to himself. The pronoun “io” is emphatic because it is stated explicitly and isolated by the pause.
segments.inferno-2-g31: “Perché” is interrogative, “why.” “Venirvi” is an infinitive with enclitic “vi,” literally “to come/go there,” used elliptically for “why should I go there?”
segments.inferno-2-g111: Disse is the passato remoto, third-person singular, of dire, used as the narrative verb introducing direct speech. The colon and dash mark the beginning of Lucia’s quoted words.
segments.inferno-2-g112: Beatrice is a vocative: Lucia is directly addressing Beatrice. The commas set the name off from the appositive description that follows.
segments.inferno-2-g113: loda is best taken as a noun, “praise,” not as a finite verb here; the phrase is an apposition to Beatrice. di Dio means “of God,” and vera agrees in gender and number with loda, giving “true praise of God.”
segments.inferno-2-g114: ché here functions as an interrogative “why,” though the listed senses do not give that value directly. soccorri is present indicative, second-person singular, addressed to Beatrice; quei means “that one,” and che t’amò tanto is a relative clause, with t’ as the elided object pronoun “you” and amò as passato remoto.
segments.inferno-2-g115: ch’ is an elided relative pronoun referring back to quei, continuing the description of the man Beatrice should help. uscì is passato remoto, “left/went out”; per te means “for your sake,” and de la is the older separated form of modern della, “from the.”
segments.inferno-2-g116: Non odi tu is a direct question with explicit tu after the verb for emphasis: “do you not hear?” la pieta del suo pianto means “the pity/pathos of his weeping,” with del joining di + il and suo referring to the man in danger.
segments.inferno-2-g117: non vedi tu parallels the previous question, again with explicit tu for emphasis. la morte che ’l combatte personifies death as something that assails him; ’l before combatte is the elided object pronoun “him,” not the article. su la is the older separated form of modern sulla, and ove introduces the relative place-clause “where the sea has no boast/superiority.”
segments.inferno-2-g118: This segment is only punctuation, marking the close of the quoted appeal before the narration continues. There are no grammatical words to analyze.
segments.inferno-2-g119: Al mondo means “in the world,” and non fur mai is a negative perfect expression: “there never were,” with fur an apocopated poetic form of furono. persone ratte a takes infinitives to express people “swift to” do something; far lor pro and fuggir lor danno are parallel infinitive phrases, with lor used possessively as “their.”
segments.inferno-2-g120: com’ is come elided before io and introduces the comparison: “as I.” It links the preceding general statement about no one being so swift to Beatrice’s own immediate action.
segments.inferno-2-g121: dopo cotai parole fatte means “after such words were made/spoken.” cotai is an archaic plural form meaning “such,” and fatte is a feminine plural past participle agreeing with parole.
segments.inferno-2-g122: venni is the passato remoto, first-person singular, of venire, despite the misleading listed lemma; it is the main verb of the comparison, “I came.” qua giù means “down here,” and del mio beato scanno means “from my blessed seat,” with del expressing origin after a verb of motion.
segments.inferno-2-g123: fidandomi is a gerund with the enclitic reflexive pronoun mi, from fidarsi, “trusting/relying.” The verb takes di, so del tuo parlare onesto means “in/of your honorable speech,” where parlare is a noun, “speech.”
segments.inferno-2-g124: ch’onora is a relative clause referring back to tuo parlare onesto: “which honors.” te is the direct object of onora, and quei ch’udito l’hanno means “those who have heard it,” with poetic inversion of l’hanno udito and l’ referring back to the speech.
segments.inferno-2-g125: “Poscia che” introduces a temporal subordinate clause, “after.” “M’ebbe ragionato” uses avere + past participle in the past anterior, literally “had spoken/reasoned to me,” with m’ as an indirect object; “questo” is the thing she has just said.
segments.inferno-2-g126: This is the main action after the temporal clause: the subject is “li occhi lucenti,” with archaic “li” for modern “gli/i.” “Lagrimando” is a gerund meaning “weeping,” and “volse” is passato remoto, “turned.”
segments.inferno-2-g127: “Per che” means “whereby/for which reason,” referring back to her tearful turning of the eyes. “Mi fece del venir più presto” means “made me quicker in coming”; “del venir” is an articulated infinitive, literally “of the coming,” used to specify in what respect he became quicker.
segments.inferno-2-g128: “E venni a te” is first-person passato remoto, “and I came to you,” continuing Virgil’s account of his response. “Così com’ ella volse” means “just as she wished”; here “volse” is an archaic/poetic form for “willed,” not “turned.”
segments.inferno-2-g129: “D’inanzi a” means “from in front of/before,” and “ti levai” is “I removed you,” with “ti” as direct object. The relative clause “che ... ti tolse” describes “quella fiera”: the beast took away from you “il corto andar del bel monte,” the short/direct way up the beautiful mountain.
segments.inferno-2-g130: “Dunque” draws an inference from all that has just been said: given such help, Virgil asks what the problem is. The colon creates a pointed pause before the direct question.
segments.inferno-2-g131: “Che è?” is a brief direct question, literally “what is it?” The verb “è” is present indicative of “essere.”
segments.inferno-2-g132: The isolated “perché” begins an urgent series of rhetorical questions. It means “why,” not “because,” in this interrogative context.
segments.inferno-2-g133: “Perché restai” asks why Dante is stopping or holding back. “Restai” is an archaic/poetic form addressed to “you” in context, even though it looks like a first-person past form in modern Italian.
segments.inferno-2-g134: This continues the repeated “perché”: “why do you harbor so much cowardice in your heart?” “Nel core” is poetic for “in the heart,” and “allette” is used in the sense of lodging or bedding something within oneself.
segments.inferno-2-g135: Another parallel question follows: “why do you not have boldness and confidence?” “Ardire” is a noun here, not an infinitive, and “hai” is present indicative, directly addressing Dante.
segments.inferno-2-g136: “Poscia che” here has a causal force, “since/given that,” introducing the reason Dante should not be afraid. “Tai” is poetic for “tali,” “such,” and “curan di te” means “care for you”; “ne la” is the older separated spelling of modern “nella.”
segments.inferno-2-g137: “E ’l” is the contraction of “e il,” “and the.” “Parlar” is a substantivized infinitive meaning “speech/words,” and “tanto ben ti promette” means that Virgil’s words promise or assure Dante so much good.
segments.inferno-2-g41: This opens a simile with the correlative pattern qual ... tal, “as/like ... so/such.” Quei che means “the one who,” disvuol is a poetic compound meaning “un-wants” or “no longer wants,” and volle is a past tense: what he formerly wanted; proposta here is a noun-like “purpose/intention.”
segments.inferno-2-g42: Sì che gives the result, “so that.” Dal cominciar uses the infinitive as a noun, “from the beginning/starting,” while si tolle is reflexive: literally “takes himself away,” hence “withdraws”; tutto works adverbially, “entirely.”
segments.inferno-2-g43: Tal answers the earlier qual and completes the comparison: “such/as that, so did I...” Mi fec’ ïo is literally “I made myself,” meaning “I became”; fec’ is an elided poetic form of feci. ’N is an apocopated form of in, and costa here means “slope/hillside,” though the supplied lemma does not match that noun sense.
segments.inferno-2-g44: Perché introduces the reason for Dante’s change of resolve: “because.” It links the completed simile to the explanation that follows.
segments.inferno-2-g45: Pensando is a gerund, “thinking” or “by thinking.” It expresses the means or process by which the speaker undermined his own resolve.
segments.inferno-2-g46: Consumai is a first-person past tense and here means “wore away, wasted, brought to nothing.” La ’mpresa is an elided form of la impresa, “the undertaking,” and the relative clause che fu nel cominciar cotanto tosta says that this undertaking “was so hard at the beginning.”
segments.inferno-2-g47: S’i’ is an elision for Se io, “if I,” although the word list separates it imperfectly. Ho ... intesa is a compound past, “I have understood,” with intesa agreeing poetically with the feminine object la parola tua, “your speech/words”; ben modifies the verb: “well, correctly.”
segments.inferno-2-g48: Rispuose is an archaic form of rispose, “replied.” The word order is inverted for poetry: the subject is quell’ ombra, “that shade,” qualified by del magnanimo, “of the noble-hearted/high-minded one.”
segments.inferno-2-g49: L’anima tua is the subject, “your soul.” È da viltade offesa is a passive-like construction: literally “is injured by cowardice,” with da marking the agent or cause.
segments.inferno-2-g50: La qual refers back to viltade, “cowardice”: “which.” Molte fïate means “many times/often,” l’omo is archaic for l’uomo, and ingombra means “encumbers/burdens”; the result clause sì che ... lo rivolve means “so that it turns him back” from an honorable undertaking.
segments.inferno-2-g51: Come introduces a comparison: cowardice turns a man back as false seeing affects an animal. Falso veder is a substantivized infinitive phrase, “false sight/perception”; quand’ ombra is compressed and poetic, with ombra functioning as “shies/takes fright at a shadow,” a sense not represented in the supplied noun senses.
segments.inferno-2-g52: Da questa tema means “from this fear”; tema is an old form used with the meaning “fear.” Acciò che is a purpose conjunction, “so that/in order that,” and tu ti solve is reflexive, “you free/loosen yourself,” with solve used as an old subjunctive form after the purpose clause.
segments.inferno-2-g53: Dirotti is not the adjective dirotto here but a fused form of dirò ti, “I will tell you.” Perch’ io venni means “why I came,” and quel ch’io ’ntesi means “what I heard”; nel primo punto che di te mi dolve means “at the first moment when I felt grief/pity concerning you.”
segments.inferno-2-g54: Io era is imperfect, “I was,” describing Virgil’s continuing state. Tra color che son sospesi means “among those who are suspended,” with color as an old shortened form of coloro, “those,” and sospesi describing the souls in a state of suspension.
segments.inferno-2-g55: Donna has no article, a common poetic usage: “a lady.” Mi chiamò means “called/summoned me,” and beata e bella are adjectives describing the lady, even though they are placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.inferno-2-g56: Tal che means “such that/so that,” giving the effect of the lady’s blessed beauty and authority. Di comandare is an infinitive complement after richiesi, and io la richiesi means “I asked her,” with la functioning as the object pronoun “her,” not as an article.
segments.inferno-2-g96: “I’” is the poetic abbreviation of “io,” and “son fatta” is a present perfect/passive form: literally “I am made.” The feminine participle “fatta” agrees with the female speaker, and “da Dio” marks the agent, “by God.”
segments.inferno-2-g97: “Sua mercé” is a parenthetical phrase meaning “by/through his mercy,” with “sua” referring back to God. “Mercé” is an archaic noun for mercy or grace, often used in this kind of adverbial expression.
segments.inferno-2-g98: “Tale” means “such” or “of such a kind” and looks forward to the following “che” clauses. It sets up the result: she has been made such that Hell’s misery and fire cannot harm her.
segments.inferno-2-g99: “Che” introduces the result clause dependent on “tale.” “La vostra miseria” is the subject, while “mi” is the object before the verb; “tange” is present tense from “tangere,” here meaning “touches/affects.”
segments.inferno-2-g100: “Né” coordinates a second negative result with the preceding clause. “D’esto ’ncendio” contracts “di esto incendio,” with archaic “esto” for “this,” and the extra “non” after “né” is a normal medieval Italian negative reinforcement rather than a cancellation.
segments.inferno-2-g101: The order “Donna è gentil nel ciel” is poetic inversion for “There is a noble lady in Heaven.” “Che” introduces a relative clause modifying “Donna,” and “si compiange di” is a pronominal expression meaning “has pity on”; “ov’” is elided “ove,” linking the obstacle to the mission on which “I send you.”
segments.inferno-2-g102: “Sì che” forms a result clause, “so that.” The object “duro giudicio” comes before the verb for poetic emphasis, while “là sù” means “up there,” in Heaven; “frange” is present tense, “breaks” or “sets aside.”
segments.inferno-2-g103: “Questa” is a pronoun meaning “this one,” referring to the noble lady just mentioned. “Chiese Lucia” uses “chiedere” with Lucia as the person appealed to or asked, and “in suo dimando” is an adverbial phrase meaning “in her request”; “disse” is passato remoto, introducing direct speech with “Or,” “Now.”
segments.inferno-2-g104: “Ha bisogno di” is the idiom “has need of.” “Il tuo fedele” uses the adjective “fedele” as a noun, “your faithful one” or “your devotee,” and “di te” completes the expression of need.
segments.inferno-2-g105: “A te” is placed before the object pronoun for emphasis: “to you.” “Lo” is the direct-object pronoun, “him,” and “raccomando” is present tense, “I commend/entrust.”
segments.inferno-2-g106: “Lucia” is the subject of the sentence, identified as Saint Lucy. The following phrase is an apposition describing her before the main verbs arrive.
segments.inferno-2-g107: “Nimica” is a feminine noun/adjective agreeing with Lucia, “enemy.” “Di ciascun crudele” uses singular “ciascun” distributively, and “crudele” is substantivized: “every cruel person.”
segments.inferno-2-g108: “Si mosse” is passato remoto of the pronominal verb “muoversi,” meaning “she moved” or “set out.” The “si” is part of the reflexive/pronominal construction, not a separate object in English.
segments.inferno-2-g109: “Venne” is passato remoto of “venire,” continuing the sequence of completed actions. “Al” contracts “a il,” “dov’” contracts “dove,” and “i’ era” means “I was,” with “era” in the imperfect to describe the location or state in the background.
segments.inferno-2-g110: “Che” resumes or explains the first-person subject: “I, who/for I.” “Mi sedea” is an archaic imperfect form of “mi sedevo,” “I was sitting,” and “l’antica Rachele” has elision of the article before “antica.”
segments.inferno-2-g57: “Lucevan” is an archaic/poetic imperfect form for modern “lucevano,” with the subject “li occhi suoi” following the verb. “Più che” forms the comparison “more than,” here meaning that her eyes shone more brightly than “la stella,” probably the morning star or a heavenly star.
segments.inferno-2-g58: “Cominciommi” is a fused poetic form: “cominciò” + “mi,” meaning “she began to me,” i.e. “she began to say to me.” “A dir” is the infinitive construction after “cominciare,” and “soave e piana” function adverbially/adjectivally to describe the manner of her speaking as gentle and plain/clear.
segments.inferno-2-g59: This prepositional phrase modifies the preceding act of speaking: she speaks “with an angelic voice.” “Angelica” agrees with feminine “voce” and gives the voice a heavenly quality.
segments.inferno-2-g60: “In sua favella” continues to describe how she speaks. “Favella” can mean speech, tongue, or language, and “sua” marks it as her own manner of speech.
segments.inferno-2-g61: This is a direct address beginning the quoted speech. “O” is vocative, and “anima cortese mantoana” means “courteous/noble Mantuan soul,” addressing Virgil by his origin, Mantua.
segments.inferno-2-g62: “Di cui” means “whose,” introducing a relative clause referring back to the Mantuan soul. “Dura” is a present-tense verb here, “endures/lasts,” though its listed lemma senses do not include that verbal meaning.
segments.inferno-2-g63: “Durerà” is future tense, continuing the idea of the previous clause: Virgil’s fame will endure. “Quanto ’l mondo” means “as long as the world,” with “’l” as elided “il”; “lontana” is poetic and reinforces the extent/duration of the world’s reach.
segments.inferno-2-g64: “L’amico mio” introduces the subject of the following verb “è impedito.” The definite article plus possessive is normal in Italian: literally “the friend my,” meaning “my friend.”
segments.inferno-2-g65: This parenthetical phrase contrasts Beatrice’s true friendship with unstable luck: he is “my friend, and not fortune’s.” “De la” is an older spelling for modern “della.”
segments.inferno-2-g66: “Ne la” is archaic spelling for modern “nella,” “in the.” “È impedito” is a passive construction, “is hindered,” and “sì nel cammin” sets up a correlative result clause with the following “che”: “so hindered in his path that...”
segments.inferno-2-g67: “Che” completes the “sì...che” result construction: he is so hindered that he has turned back. “Vòlt’ è” is “è volto,” a contracted past participle with auxiliary “essere,” and “per paura” gives the cause, “through fear.”
segments.inferno-2-g68: “Temo che” takes the subjunctive, here “sia ... smarrito,” meaning “I fear that he may have gone astray.” The “non” after a verb of fearing is pleonastic/expletive in older Italian and is not translated as a real negative.
segments.inferno-2-g69: “Ch’” introduces a result clause after “sì smarrito”: he may be so lost that Beatrice has risen too late to help. “Mi sia ... levata” is a reflexive/pronominal perfect subjunctive, literally “I have raised myself,” meaning “I have risen/set out.”
segments.inferno-2-g70: “Per quel ch’i’ ho ... udito” means “because of/from what I have heard,” with “ch’i’” standing for “che io.” “Ho udito” is a perfect-tense construction, and “di lui” means “about him,” while “nel cielo” locates the report in heaven.
segments.inferno-2-g71: “Or” means “now,” introducing an urgent command. “Movi” is an imperative form meaning “move” or “set out,” addressed to Virgil.
segments.inferno-2-g72: The two “con” phrases give the means by which Virgil is to help: with his “parola ornata,” his elegant or adorned speech, and with whatever is necessary. “Ciò c’ha mestieri” means “that which is needed”; the written “c’” represents elided “che,” not the pronoun “ci” as listed.
segments.inferno-2-g73: “L’aiuta” means “help him,” where “l’” is an object pronoun, not the article. “Sì ch’” introduces a purpose/result clause, “so that,” and “i’” is elided “io”; “ne sia consolata” means “I may be comforted by it/on account of it.”
segments.inferno-2-g74: “I’ son” is poetic for “io sono,” “I am.” “Che ti faccio andare” is a relative clause referring to Beatrice; “faccio andare” is a causative construction, “I make/cause you to go.”
segments.inferno-2-g75: “Vegno” is an archaic form of “vengo,” “I come.” “Del loco ove” means “from the place where/to which,” and “tornar disio” uses poetic word order with the infinitive before the verb: “I desire to return.”
segments.inferno-2-g76: “Amor” is the subject placed first for emphasis and may be felt almost as personified Love. “Mi mosse” means “moved me” or “set me in motion,” but the provided senses for “mosse” do not include this verbal meaning.
segments.inferno-2-g77: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “amor”: Love is what makes her speak. “Mi fa parlare” is another causative construction, “makes me speak.”
segments.inferno-2-g78: “Quando sarò” is a future temporal clause: “when I shall be.” “Dinanzi al segnor mio” means “before my Lord,” with “segnor” referring reverently to God in Beatrice’s speech.
segments.inferno-2-g79: “Di te” is fronted for emphasis: “of you/about you.” “Mi loderò ... a lui” is an idiomatic reflexive form meaning that she will speak well of him, i.e. praise or commend him, to her Lord.

sentences.inferno-2-s41: Now go, for one sole will belongs to us both: you are my guide, you my lord, and you my master.
sentences.inferno-2-s42: Thus I spoke to him; and after he had set out, I entered upon the deep and wild path.
sentences.inferno-2-s10: I am not Aeneas, I am not Paul; neither I nor anyone else believes me worthy of this.
sentences.inferno-2-s11: Therefore, if I give myself over to the journey, I fear that the coming may be rash.
sentences.inferno-2-s12: You are wise; you understand better than I can explain.
sentences.inferno-2-s38: As little flowers, bent down and closed by the night frost, after the sun whitens them, all straighten themselves, open on their stems, so did I become from my weary strength, and so much good courage ran to my heart that I began like a bold person: “Oh, compassionate was she who came to my aid!
sentences.inferno-2-s39: and courteous were you, who immediately obeyed the true words that she offered you!
sentences.inferno-2-s40: You, with your words, have so disposed my heart with desire to come that I have returned to my first purpose.
sentences.inferno-2-s21: Then she fell silent, and then I began: “O lady of virtue, you alone through whom the human race surpasses everything contained within that heaven which has the smallest of its circles, your command so pleases me that to obey it, even if it were already done, seems late to me; you need do no more than reveal your desire to me.”
sentences.inferno-2-s22: But tell me the reason why you do not beware of descending down here into this center from the vast place to which you burn to return.”
sentences.inferno-2-s23: “Since you wish to know so deeply, I will tell you briefly,” she answered me, “why I do not fear to come in here.”
sentences.inferno-2-s24: One must fear only those things that have the power to do harm to others; not the others, for they are not frightening.
sentences.inferno-2-s1: The day was departing, and the dark air was relieving the living creatures that are on earth from their labors; and I alone was preparing myself to endure the struggle both of the journey and of the pity, which the memory that does not err will recount.
sentences.inferno-2-s2: O Muses, O lofty genius, now help me; O memory that wrote down what I saw, here your excellence will be shown.
sentences.inferno-2-s3: I began: “Poet who guide me, consider my strength, whether it is capable, before you entrust me to the arduous passage.”
sentences.inferno-2-s4: You say that the father of Silvius, still corruptible, went to the immortal realm, and was there bodily.
sentences.inferno-2-s5: Therefore, if the adversary of every evil was gracious to him, considering the lofty outcome that was to issue from him, and who he was and what he was, this does not seem unworthy to a man of understanding; for in the Empyrean Heaven he was chosen to be the father of noble Rome and of her empire: that city and that empire, to tell the truth, were established for the holy place where the successor of the greater Peter sits.
sentences.inferno-2-s6: Through this journey, for which you give him praise, he learned things that were the cause of his victory and of the papal mantle.
sentences.inferno-2-s7: Afterwards the Chosen Vessel went there, to bring back comfort for that faith which is the beginning of the way of salvation.
sentences.inferno-2-s29: She said: “Beatrice, true praise of God, why do you not help the one who loved you so much that, for your sake, he left the common rank?”
sentences.inferno-2-s30: Do you not hear the pity in his weeping, do you not see the death that assails him upon the flood where the sea has no superiority?
sentences.inferno-2-s32: Never in the world were people so swift to seek their own profit or to flee their own harm as I was, when, after such words were spoken, I came down here from my blessed seat, trusting in your honorable speech, which honors you and those who have heard it.
sentences.inferno-2-s33: After she had told me this, she turned her shining eyes away, weeping; because of that she made me swifter in coming.
sentences.inferno-2-s34: And I came to you just as she wished: I removed you from before that beast which took from you the short way up the beautiful mountain.
sentences.inferno-2-s36: Why, why do you hold back, why do you harbor so much cowardice in your heart, why do you not have boldness and confidence, since such three blessed ladies care for you in the court of heaven, and my words promise you so much good?
sentences.inferno-2-s13: And like one who no longer wants what he once wanted and, because of new thoughts, changes his purpose, so that he withdraws entirely from beginning it, such did I become on that dark slope, because, by thinking, I brought to nothing the undertaking that had been so hard at the start.
sentences.inferno-2-s14: “If I have understood your words correctly,” replied that shade of the noble-hearted one, “your soul is harmed by cowardice; and this often so encumbers a man that it turns him back from an honorable undertaking, as a false sight does to a beast when it shies at a shadow.”
sentences.inferno-2-s15: To free you from this fear, I will tell you why I came and what I heard at the first moment when I grieved for you.
sentences.inferno-2-s16: I was among those who are suspended, and a blessed and beautiful lady called me, such that I asked her to command me.
sentences.inferno-2-s25: I have been made by God, through his mercy, such that your misery does not affect me, nor does any flame of this fire assail me.
sentences.inferno-2-s26: There is a noble lady in Heaven who has pity on this hindrance for which I send you, so that she breaks stern judgment up above.
sentences.inferno-2-s27: This lady appealed to Lucia in her request and said: “Now your faithful one has need of you, and I commend him to you.”
sentences.inferno-2-s28: Lucia, enemy of every cruel one, set out and came to the place where I was, I who was sitting with ancient Rachel.
sentences.inferno-2-s17: Her eyes shone brighter than the star; and she began to speak to me, gentle and clear, with an angelic voice, in her own speech: “O courteous Mantuan soul, whose fame still endures in the world and will endure as long as the world lasts, my friend—and not the friend of fortune—is so hindered on the deserted slope in his path that he has turned back through fear; and I fear that he may already be so far astray that I have risen too late to aid him, from what I have heard of him in heaven.”
sentences.inferno-2-s18: Now go, and with your elegant speech and with whatever is needed for his survival, help him so that I may be comforted by it.
sentences.inferno-2-s19: I am Beatrice, who make you go; I come from the place to which I long to return; Love moved me, which makes me speak.
sentences.inferno-2-s20: When I am before my Lord, I shall often praise you to him.
